data.title: Sirius XM: Investors Should Listen To The Strong Technicals And Attractive Valuation
data.description:
Thesis

Sirius XM Holdings Inc. (NASDAQ:SIRI) recently made headlines as Warren Buffett's Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B) raised its stake in the company to 35.4% after acquiring another 2.3 million shares. In the below analysis, I determine that the technicals are very strong for SIRI stock, with the vast majority of signals showing further strength is likely. For the fundamentals, while revenue growth is currently weak, the company did report promising EPS and FCF figures. Furthermore, 2025 full-year guidance was also overall strong and with the P/FCF ratio failing to reflect the major improvement in the fundamentals, I conclude that the stock is undervalued currently. Therefore, I believe Buffett's decision to purchase more SIRI stock is justified and investors should consider buying as well.

Technicals

Charting

Trends

SIRI stock recently entered into a near term uptrend and in the process, it also broke above a longer-term downtrend that dates back to August. The stock did experience significant resistance at the downtrend line before finally able to break above. However, I highlighted the decrease in volume recently and so there is still some potential that this could be a false breakout.

Support

In addition to the former downtrend line and current uptrend line being support, there are two other noteworthy support levels. At around the 25 level, the stock experienced significant support there in November and also a bit earlier this month before the break above the downtrend line. The other support area is at around 22.5 and represents strong support back in September and October while being resistance in mid-January.

Resistance

There is only one major resistance area in this timeframe, and it is at around 28. This price level represents a major downside gap that occurred in December and given the high volume on that day, its significance is high. In addition, this level was also resistance in September, October, and early November and so this area has consistently been resistance before.

Takeaway

I believe it is clear that the chart is very positive for SIRI. The stock is in a near term uptrend that saw the stock break above the longer-term downtrend. There is also more support than there is resistance, and so overall the technical setup here is a bullish one for SIRI.

Moving Averages

Simple Moving Average (SMA)

The 50-day SMA has remained below the 200-day SMA for the past six months as there has been no crossovers. This reflects that the stock has experienced sustained longer-term bearishness. However, the gap did close significantly in November and December and has been consistent ever since, showing that bearish momentum has been somewhat contained. The stock currently trades between the SMAs and may be experiencing resistance at the 200-day SMA.

Exponential Moving Average (EMA)

The 9 day EMA had a bullish crossover with the 21 day EMA in late January, a clear signal of near term strength in the stock. The gap between the EMAs expanded significantly initially but currently seems to be at a consistent level, showing that while bullishness is strong, it is not accelerating at this point. The stock trades above both EMAs reflecting immediate term strength.

Bollinger Bands

The stock broke above the Bollinger Bands' midline in late January on high volume as a bullish signal. This was also an early signal that a near term uptrend could be developing, and also showed that the longer-term downtrend was weakening significantly. Expect the midline to act as support moving forward.

Takeaway

Despite the SMAs still being in a bearish state overall, I argue that the moving averages are net bullish for SIRI stock. The SMAs show that long term bearish momentum has been somewhat contained, the EMAs reflect robust near term bullishness, and the Bollinger Bands provided a key signal of strength even before the stock broke above the downtrend line.

Indicators

Moving Average Convergence Divergence (MACD)

There were multiple false crossovers for the MACD in early January but the MACD ultimately pulled away from the signal line for a true bullish crossover in mid January. Since then, gap has overall expanded but there has been inconsistency as there were periods of contraction as well, showing that it hasn't been all smooth sailing for the bulls. Despite the stock being nowhere near six months highs, the MACD is at a six-month high currently, showing that there is strength building in SIRI.

Relative Strength Index (RSI)

The RSI is currently at 66.83 after just receding from an over 70 reading. This shows that the bulls are still in control of the stock by a large margin, despite surrendering some control to the bears. The RSI has been in a uptrend since the mid-December low, showing broad strengthening in the stock. In addition, like the MACD, the RSI just recorded a six-month-high and so clearly there is strength building.

Stochastics

The last few crossovers in the stochastics were generally false signals as the %K did not pull away from the %D. Both lines remained in the 80+ zone as of late, showing sustained bullishness in the stock. The last meaningful crossover occurred in late January as the %K crossed above the %D for a bullish crossover. Both lines then also surged above the 50 level as another signal that the balance has tipped in the bulls favour.

Takeaway

In my view, these indicators show the technical outlook for SIRI stock is bright. The MACD is in an overall bullish state, the RSI has been in an uptrend, and the stochastics has remained consistently in the 80+ zone as of late. Furthermore, the MACD and RSI show that while the stock is not yet at six months highs, there is some extraordinary longer-term strength building underneath the surface with both of those indicators recording their own six month highs.

Fundamentals

Earnings

Quarterly Results

SIRI released their 2024 Q4 earnings at the end of January and showed mixed results as a whole. They reported revenues of $2.188 billion, down from the prior year period's $2.287 billion and a GAAP diluted EPS of $0.83, up from $0.67 YoY. Free cash flow also showed promising signs for the company as they reported $516 million, a large improvement from the year ago quarter's $402 million. As you can see in the above chart, revenue growth is at very poor levels compared to the past three years and is relatively deep into negative territory. The continued decline in revenues should be concerning for investors. However, the FCF figures should inspire some confidence as growth has hit three year highs after a dramatic increase in their Q4. Furthermore, both the revenue and GAAP EPS results surpassed expectations as they beat by $17.98 million and $0.12 respectively, demonstrating strong performance.

Guidance

SIRI provided full year 2025 guidance but did not specifically for Q1. They are expecting 2025 revenues of approximately $8.5 billion. With total revenues in 2024 being $8.699 billion, this guidance implies a YoY revenue decline of 2.3%. For 2024, the $8.699 billion figure was a 2.8% decline YoY from $8.953 billion for 2023. Therefore, the company is expecting revenue declines to moderate somewhat in the coming year. They also provided a FCF guidance figure of around $1.15 billion. FCF for 2024 was $1.015 billion, and so this estimate implies 2025 YoY FCF growth of 13.3%. FCF for 2023 was $1.182 billion and so for 2024, they had a YoY decline in FCF of 14.1% and so 2025 is a major improvement for this figure. Overall, I believe 2025 guidance is strong as it implies that revenue declines will moderate modestly, and FCF growth will return to significantly positive figures. Investors should be encouraged by these projections.

Valuation

Price To Sales Ratio (P/S)

The P/S ratio is currently at rock bottom levels compared to the past three years. Its current reading of 1.107 is far below average and represents major multiple contraction since the January 2024 levels of about 2.4. Despite this contraction, I believe the P/S ratio generally shows fair valuation for SIRI stock. As discussed above, revenue growth is currently at very poor levels compared to the past few years and is currently in negative figures. Full year 2025 guidance shows that revenue declines will likely moderate but growth would still be at overall under average levels. Therefore, the depressed level in the P/S ratio is not unjustified as it reflects the financials of the company rather well. The Communication Services sector median P/S ratio is currently 1.33 and so SIRI is at a moderate discount to peers. However, given their negative growth, this could be justified.

Price To Free Cash Flow Ratio (P/FCF)

The P/FCF ratio is also at very low levels compared to the past three years and is far below average. Its current reading of 9.503 is miles below the 17.5 levels seen at the start of 2024. I believe this ratio show significant undervaluation in SIRI stock. As discussed above, FCF growth hit a three year high for their Q4 as a result of a sharp rebound. 2025 guidance also shows that in full-year terms, FCF growth is expected to surge from negative figures in 2024 to around +13% for 2025. While this is below Q4's FCF growth rate, it would still be very strong levels in relation to SIRI's past FCF data. While this is the case, the P/FCF ratio is quite near three year lows and much below average. This is a major disconnect between the ratio and the fundamentals, leading me to believe that it demonstrates material undervaluation.

Takeaway

I conclude that SIRI stock is moderately undervalued at current levels. The P/S ratio shows fair valuation overall as it reflects the state of revenue growth well and is at a justifiable discount to the sector. However, for the P/FCF ratio, it is highly disconnected from FCF growth as the major improvements in the fundamentals should justify a much higher level in the ratio. Therefore, overall I believe SIRI is currently moderately undervalued.

Conclusion

In the above analysis, I have determined that the technicals are highly in the bulls' favour with broad signals of strength present. For the fundamentals, Q4 results were slightly mixed with EPS and FCF showing strong results but revenue being weak. Guidance for 2025 also seems strong as they project YoY improvements over 2024. For the valuation, after considering the P/S and P/FCF evaluations together, I believe the stock is currently moderately undervalued and so it is no surprise that Buffett is picking up more shares. In conclusion, due to the bullish technicals and overall favourable fundamental setup, I initiate SIRI stock at a buy rating.

data.title: Unemployment Claims Up 5K, Higher Than Expected
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Initial jobless claims measure the number of people who file for unemployment for the first time in a given week. In the week ending February 15th, initial jobless claims were at a seasonally adjusted level of 219,000. This represents an increase of 5,000 from the previous week's figure. The latest reading was higher than the 214,000 forecast.

Here is the opening statement from the Department of Labor:

In the week ending February 15, the advance figure for seasonally adjusted initial claims was 219,000, an increase of 5,000 from the previous week's revised level. The previous week's level was revised up by 1,000 from 213,000 to214,000. The 4-week moving average was 215,250, a decrease of 1,000 from the previous week's revised average. The previous week's average was revised up by 250 from 216,000 to 216,250.

Here is a closer look at the series since 2007, with a callout to the past 12 months. The four-week moving average, which smooths short-term fluctuations to highlight the overall trend, currently stands at 215,250 - a decrease of 1,000 from the previous week.

For an even shorter timeline, the chart below shows initial unemployment claims starting in 2022.

As we can see, there's a good bit of volatility in this indicator, which is why the four-week moving average is a more useful number than the weekly data. Here is the complete data series dating back to 1967.

Outside of the COVID spike, initial unemployment claims have never been greater than 700,000 for a given week, making the chart above less useful for identifying trends. To address this, we've adjusted the y-axis on the chart below to provide a closer view of the data, minimizing the impact of the COVID surge.

Notice the relationship between recessions and the rise in weekly unemployment claims. To no surprise, the 4-week moving average begins to rise at or before the start of a recession and peaks around its conclusion.

The headline unemployment insurance data - and the charts above - are seasonally adjusted. But what does the non-seasonally adjusted data look like? The chart below highlights its extreme volatility, as shown by the green dots. The four-week moving average helps reveal the recurring seasonal patterns, such as the regular spikes in January.

Again, in this next chart, we've adjusted the y-axis to get a zoomed in view of the series where the COVID spike isn't as prominent.

Because of the extreme volatility of the non-adjusted weekly data, we can add a 52-week moving average to give a better sense of the secular trends. The chart below also has a linear regression through the data (red line), which we are currently below.

Continuing Unemployment Claims

Continuing unemployment claims are the number of people who have already filed for unemployment and have continued to claims benefits for a subsequent week.

Unlike initial unemployment claims, continuing claims are not considered a leading economic indicator and are a less important measure of economic health. Additionally, data for continuing claims has a one-week lag compared to initial claims

In the week ending February 8th, continuing jobless claims were at a seasonally adjusted level of 1,869,000. This represents an increase of 24,000 from the previous week's figure. The latest reading was lower than the 1,870,000 forecast.

The advance seasonally adjusted insured unemployment rate was 1.2 percent for the week ending February 8, unchanged from the previous week's unrevised rate. The advance number for seasonally adjusted insured unemployment during the week ending February 8 was 1,869,000, an increase of 24,000 from the previous week's revised level. The previous week's level was revised down by 5,000 from 1,850,000 to 1,845,000. The 4-week moving average was 1,862,500, a decrease of 7,750 from the previous week's revised average. The previous week's average was revised down by 1,250 from 1,871,500 to 1,870,250.

Here is the data series dating back to 1967.

Once again, here is a zoomed in view so that the COVID-spike is not as prominent.And lastly, here is a chart starting in 2022 to see the more recent trends in continuing claims.

Original Post

data.title: Amerigo Resources: Shareholder Value Is Well-Founded With Consistent Production, Costs And Robust Metal Prospects
data.description:
A “Hold” Rating for Amerigo Resources Ltd.

This analysis assigns a "Hold" rating to Amerigo Resources Ltd. (OTCQX:ARREF) shares. We gave the same rating in the previous article dated November 01, 2024.

At the time of writing, this company's stock has performed in line with our previous recommendation: holdings in Amerigo Resources allowed investors to achieve a total return of 7.00% (including a 5.14% stock appreciation and a 1.86% dividend yield) versus the S&P 500's return of +7.06%. The drivers were rising copper and molybdenum prices, as well as the company's consistent production and cost performance.

The company was consistent with the payment of quarterly dividends: It paid CAD 0.03 per share on December 20, 2024. This analysis assumes that the company is well positioned to return more value to its shareholders in the future.

About Amerigo Resources Ltd. and Its Metal Production in Chile

In our previous article we wrote:

“Amerigo Resources produces copper and molybdenum from the fresh and historic tailings of its 100% owned Minera Valle Central ("MVC"). Copper is the company's primary source of income. The tailings come from previous mining at the underground El Teniente mine in Chile.

Additional base metals are extracted, mainly by subjecting the mineral material from previous mining at the underground El Teniente mine in Chile to further processing.

El Teniente is owned by Codelco (which is short for “The National Copper Corporation of Chile”), a Chilean state-owned copper mining company.”

Amerigo Resources is based in Vancouver, Canada, and Chile is by far

“the world's leading copper producer”, having “produced an estimated 5.3 million metric tons of copper in 2023.”, writes Statista.

As a location for mineral mining and exploration activities, Chile implies a “low-risk factor”, according to the Sprott ESG Mining Risk Heat Map 2025.

The Reason Behind the “Hold” Rating

Investors are advised to continue to hold Amerigo Resources' US-listed shares in their portfolio as 2025 offers hope for another promising year for the company's profitability, a key driver for US equities. The positive outlook for Amerigo's profitability is supported by the continued increase in the price of copper, the company's main source of revenue, and by the strengthening of the US dollar ("USD") against the Chilean peso ("CLP"), which has a positive impact on operating costs translated from the local currency to the USD.

Rising copper prices and the significantly positive cost impact of a stronger USD will effectively offset the lower copper production the company is targeting for 2025 compared to 2024.

The Outlook For The Copper and Molybdenum Price

Copper will continue to be used as a key component in electrification, the manufacture of electric vehicles and their batteries. Copper is also used in the construction of technologies that enable the exploitation of renewable energy sources. The high demand for copper, leading to significant upward pressure on the price per unit, is also driven by increasing hype surrounding the global proliferation of AI data centers.

The company wrote:

“[…] market consensus continues to support rising copper prices due to concentrate supply deficits in 2025, […]”.

With available copper barely sufficient to meet demand, which we believe will continue to be driven higher by strong factors, the price per pound of copper will face astonishing upward pressure.

Perhaps short-term copper price forecasts are influenced by the possibility of Russian copper returning to the Western market if the US manages to end the war between Russia and Ukraine.

Thus, analysts at Trading Economics expect copper futures (HG1:COM), which were at $4.59/lb at the time of writing, up 75.87% over the past five years due to strong demand factors against the limited supply just mentioned, to trade at $4.33/lb by the end of this quarter, then recover to $4.528/lb within $4.528/lb. Click on the “Forecast” section on the Trading Economics website.

According to the longer-term price forecast, the latest available forecast is from BMI (a subsidiary of Fitch Solutions) in early October 2024 and assumes that prices will “reach $17,000/tonne in 2033.”, Seeking Alpha wrote. This equates to about $7.71/lb since a tonne of copper weighs about 2,204.64 pounds.

Molybdenum is a silver-grey metal, a good conductor of heat and electricity. In nature, it occurs mainly in the form of molybdenite and wulfenite. After extraction, it is used both in pure form and in alloys as a carrier for lamp filaments, in electronic tubes and as a heating element in resistance furnaces. It is also used in rocket technology and the ceramics industry. Therefore, molybdenum is in great demand because of the hype surrounding the global plans to build an ecologically sustainable economy through increasing electrification and the arms race. The robust demand for moly tends to drive up the price.

As for molybdenum prices, according to Trading Economics, this metal has been “at 469 since the beginning of 2025”, the website wrote, and its analysts expect it to trade at CNY 473.92/kg before March 31, 2024, and CNY 489.03/kg before end February 2026. Click on the “Forecast” section on the Trading Economics website.

CNY, the abbreviation for Chinese Yuan Renminbi, is the official currency of the People’s Republic of China.

At the time of writing: 469 CNY/kg = 64.5077 USD/kg (or USD29.260/lb ); 473.92 CNY/kg = 65.1844 USD/kg (or USD29.567/lb); 489.03 CNY/kg = 67.2627 USD/kg (or USD30.510/lb).

About Future Profitability: Rising Metal Prices, Higher Production and Lower Costs, Including the Positive Impact of a Stronger US Dollar vs. Chilean peso

The company wrote in its report, “Amerigo Reports 2024 Operational Results & Provides 2025 Guidance”:

“While market consensus continues to support rising copper prices due to concentrate supply deficits in 2025, Amerigo has again taken a conservative approach to selecting economic assumptions for its annual budget, including an average market price of $4.15/lb for copper, $21/lb for molybdenum and an exchange rate of 940 Chilean pesos (“CLP”) to USD 1 (collectively, the “Assumptions”).”

The above figures refer to metal prices. Aside from the high probability that significantly higher metal prices than Amerigo's conservative estimates will have a positive impact on company profitability - an important driver of U.S.-listed stocks - there are also some cost considerations to be made (as we will see a bit later in this section) regarding the costs estimated by the company.

Concerning 2025 costs, the company provides the following estimate: Amerigo's normalized cash costs are expected to be $1.93/lb in 2025, compared to $1.87 in 2024. This reflects the greater impact of lower year-over-year production in 2025 and higher energy and production inputs (including labor costs) than the benefits of lower treating and refining costs.

The expected margin for 2025 (prices minus cost forecasts) must be compared with a reference year to get an idea of whether the situation will improve or worsen. The year 2023 can be used as a comparison year because we do not yet have the financial results for 2024. These will be published on February 26, 2025.

In the reference year 2023, the company had cash costs of $2.17/lb but realized an average copper price of $3.86/lb. The company produced 57.6 million pounds of copper in 2023 (a 10% decrease from the previous year). Combining these elements enabled Amerigo to achieve a certain level of profitability. This put Amerigo on a solid footing to return $17.2 million to its shareholders (85% in dividends and 15% in share repurchases) and maintain a strong balance sheet with a reduction in net debt despite the company's unusually capital-intensive year.

While shareholder value creation and a healthy balance sheet are trends that are having a positive impact on US stock market sentiment, their trigger, the profit margin, will likely be stronger in 2025. With cash costs expected to be lower at $1.93/lb in 2025 (than the reference year), and copper likely to trade higher than the company's conservative estimate of $4.15/lb (and reference year), the company's operating margin is well positioned to drive optimism in market sentiment through strong shareholder value creation and a healthy balance sheet. We believe this also because of the following cost considerations: First of all, Amerigo's cost estimates take into account inflationary pressures, but probably not the further decline in future inflation following the sharp cooling of core consumer prices from 10.88% in August 2022 to 4.10% in January 2025. To bring down inflation, the Central Bank of Chile has been aggressively increasing interest rate policy in recent years (0.5% in June 2021; 11.25% in June 2023; 5% in January 2025). Second, it is also important to note that the majority of production costs are paid in Chile's local currency, and Amerigo has a conservative approach to the exchange rate of the Chilean peso ("CLP") against the US dollar ("USD"): the company estimates that the CLP is worth 940 to one USD, but the USD is strengthening against the CLP and has been in the range of 947 to 1,012 over the past 3 months. Lower operating costs in USD will have a decorative effect on profitability and help build positive market sentiment around the stock.

Thus for 2025, the company forecasts:

“Projected 2025 EBITDA is expected to be $66.7 million, said the company. Each $0.10/lb increase in copper price up to $4.80/lb would increase EBITDA by approximately $3.0 million.”

But the company may be on track to hit higher EBITDA.

EBITDA was$34.6 million in 2023. The year 2023 is our reference year in our analysis, as the 2024 financial results will be released much later this month.

Strong Long-Term Performance: Share Price Appreciation and Paid Dividends and Buybacks

Sustained profitability is a prerequisite to maintaining a healthy financial position and increasing the company's long-term metal production potential ahead of bright metal price prospects. This means that Amerigo remains a firm anchor in the growth potential of Chile's metal resources and benefits from the tailings from the exploitation of the large copper and molybdenum deposits controlled by state-owned giant Codelco.

Amerigo Resources wrote:

“The Company repaid $9.8 million in debt (2023: $7.1 million in debt and lease repayments) and returned $21.2 million to shareholders through dividends and share buybacks (2023: $17.3 million).”

The company added:

“On December 31, 2024, Amerigo’s cash position was $35.9 million ($10.8 million higher than September 30, 2024), and restricted cash was $4.4 million ($2.3 million lower than September 30, 2024). Outstanding bank debt was $11.5 million (a decrease of $4.0 million from September 30, 2024).”

Amerigo Resources' balance sheet has an Altman Z Score of 2.67 according to Seeking Alpha. This means that there is some risk of bankruptcy in the next few years. But the prospects of lower costs amid cooling inflation and a stronger US dollar, along with consistent production and a good metal price outlook mean that the risk is likely to decline going forward. If the ratio is above 3.0, the risk is zero. If the ratio is between 1.8 and 3.0, the risk is high to low. If the ratio is below 1.8, the risk of insolvency is very high.

By holding these shares for the long term, investors have already achieved a whopping 373.31% return, just from the increase in market value of Amerigo Resources Ltd.'s US-listed shares over the last five years. These have outperformed the SPDR® S&P 500® ETF Trust (SPY) (+83.37%) over the same period. The SPY is used as a benchmark to assess the performance of the whole US stock market.

The stock also has a forward dividend yield (“FWD”) of 6.53%, compared to the S&P 500’s yield of 1.22% at the time of this writing.

The Share Price Is In the Upper Price-Cycle, So Investors Should Wait for a Significant Weakness Before Buying Shares

At the time of writing, U.S.-listed shares of Amerigo Resources Ltd. traded at $1.29 apiece, giving it a market capitalization of $213.38 million.

The shares are trading high as they are above the MA Ribbon and above the midpoint of $1.225 per share of the 52-week range of $0.93 to $1.52 per share.

The 14-day Relative Strength Indicator of 67.74x indicates that overbought levels are very close and the room for further movement upwards is very limited. Investors interested in the shareholder value creation of this stock due to rising profitability expectations would be better off waiting until the stock price experiences significant weakness. A lower share price is always possible, as Amerigo Resources shares are as volatile as copper and molybdenum prices.

Copper and other base metals fell sharply in late 2024, triggered by Donald Trump's threats to impose “tariffs on goods from Mexico, Canada and China.”, Seeking Alpha wrote. Headwinds for metals, including copper and moly, pushed Amerigo Resources shares to a significant low in the following days. With Trump, the potential for a trade war is always alive, which does not bode well for metals prices in the short term. As such concerns arise from time to time, the next one could create the conditions for another better entry point into Amerigo Resources shares.

Conclusion

Amerigo Resources is a copper and molybdenum producer that processes tailings from Codelco's El Teniente underground mine in Chile, the world's largest copper producer.

Dividends and buyback plans are well-positioned to benefit from increased earnings amid a strong outlook for metal prices, along with the company being consistent with production and operating costs. A higher outlook for copper and moly will also have a positive impact on Amerigo Resources shares directly.

Dividends, buybacks, and a solid financial position are driven by strong earnings. Metal prices are rising, while a strong US dollar against the Chilean peso and falling inflation will help to reduce the company's costs in US dollars.

The stock is trading high, so investors may want to wait for weakness before adding to their positions. With Trump threatening to impose tariffs on metals, potentially leading to a decline in Amerigo Resources' share price, a weaker stock price is always a possibility.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Municipal Update: Key Themes And Allocation Tilts
data.description:
Over the last few months, the municipal market has been inundated with new developments. In this article, we catch up on the key new themes such as the funding freeze, impact of tariffs on interest rates, the CA wildfires and more. We also highlight some of our allocations and where we see value in the broader tax-exempt market.

Key Themes

A number of key themes have hit the municipal market in the last couple of months.

The first is the arrival of tariffs. So far, we have a 10% tariff on Chinese goods along with a 25% tariff on steel and aluminum which have already gone into effect while 25% tariffs on Canada and Mexico have been postponed for 30 days. There are at least two channels in which tariffs will impact Munis - growth and inflation.

When the first news of tariffs came in over the weekend, the yield curve flattened, i.e. short-term rates rose while longer-term rates fell. The signal from the market was that inflation was likely to go up (hence the rise in short-term rates) while growth would suffer (hence, the drop in longer-term rates). PIMCO estimates that US inflation could rise by 0.8% while growth could be reduced by 1.2%.

Of course, we don't know how these two factors will play out in reality. The actual tariffs themselves are highly uncertain with respect to size, breadth, length and any exclusions. We also don't know with certainty how tariffs would translate into growth and inflation and how the Fed will react to them. What we can say is that the likely range around interest rates over the medium term has increased. If there is a positive here, it's that real interest rates are fairly elevated and this provides some margin of safety for longer-duration assets like Munis.

The second key theme is the federal funding freeze announced the other week. Although funding provided directly to individuals as well as Medicare and Social Security was not affected, state and local governments scrambled to decipher which of their programs might be impacted. The OMB then clarified matters by also exempting Medicare and SNAP. The OMB then rescinded the directive, however the White House press secretary said that the recession was of the memo, not of the funding freeze itself. According to The Hill, It appears that there was a behind-the-scenes pushback from Republican lawmakers after they were "flooded with calls from community leaders who expressed confusion and concern about losing federal grants".

The general view, based on the Impoundment Control Act, is that the executive branch is prohibited from cancelling spending on its own - the President can only propose rescission to Congress and/or put spending on hold for no more than 45 days while Congress considers the proposal.

The overall grants to states and local governments total over $1trn and includes everything from K-12 education, Highways, Medicaid etc.

According to Pew Charitable Trusts, 36% of state revenues are derived from federal funds with a number of states at above 45%.

This is going to be one of the key focus areas for Munis. So far, the market has not reacted a whole lot. Most likely, this will take its time going through the Courts.

Another theme, for California investors at least, is the wildfires. At the moment, the three active fires in LA County, comprising over 50,000 acres burned, are nearly all contained. The market expects the impacted credits to remain resilient given the history of other natural disasters in the US as well as the likelihood of federal aid.

The Los Angeles Department of Water & Power remains in focus. The state's inverse condemnation rule means the state utility could be liable for damage caused to property owners. Although there is yet no evidence of LADWP's equipment causing any of the fires, property owners could allege that the water system failed, leading to damage to their properties. Separately, the S&P revised its outlook to negative on the Pasadena Water & Power as well as the Glendale Water & Power electric revenue bonds in view of potential litigation.

Finally, tax exemption remains a risk and a potential pay-for for the extension of TCJA. The laundry list of all the potential pay-fors was released by the House Ways and Means Committee in January and included the elimination of the nonprofit status of hospitals, the elimination of the tax preference for Private Activity Bonds, BABs and other non-municipal bonds as well as the elimination of the Munis tax exemption. The latter would generate $250bn, only slightly below the elimination of the hospital nonprofit status.

The general consensus here is that there are enough senior lawmakers in key positions to keep the tax exemption in place. That said, the risk has risen somewhat and should not be dismissed outright.

Muni Valuations

The absolute yield of municipal bonds is very attractive by historical standards, even if spreads over Treasuries are not very high. With the yield curve now having disinverted, longer-duration assets like Munis are less of a disadvantage relative to floating-rate assets.

While the Treasury yield curve is relatively flat, the Muni yield curve is strongly upward sloping. This means that it makes sense to extend duration as well as take leveraged exposure via CEFs. That said, unleveraged exposure will still result in more efficient income generation.

The key chart is the one below that shows the value of tax exemption. This is simply the difference between municipal bond yield and its tax-equivalent yield at the highest marginal tax bracket. Because nominal yields are high, so is the value of the tax exemption.

On the CEF front, discounts are still relatively compelling. The fact that yields are relatively high also means that each 1% of discount generates a higher additional yield for investors than if yields were lower.

Stance And Takeaways

We follow a four-pronged approach to our municipal allocation.

The first is unleveraged CEFs. These funds are attractive because they generate highly efficient income. This is because for a typical leveraged muni CEF, the vast bulk of the income generated by leveraged assets (typically about a third of total assets) goes to pay for leverage costs and fund expenses, even with the upwardly sloping Muni yield curve. In this bucket, we continue to like the Nuveen Select Maturities Municipal Fund (NIM), trading at a current yield of 3.6% and an 8.5% discount.

The fund has a net investment income yield on price (what we call the "covered yield") of 3.6% or only 10% below the average in the Muni CEF sector despite carrying a third fewer assets than the typical Muni CEF. EIM also has one of the lowest fees in the sector, which contributes to its efficient income. In our view, this is the most attractive, lower risk way to allocate to Munis while getting the benefit of active management.

In the second bucket, we put CEFs with ongoing tender offers, such as the Eaton Vance Municipal Bond Fund (EIM) and the BlackRock MuniVest Fund (MVF). These funds hold tender periodic tender offers, which can provide significant upside for investors. EIM has at least one more tender offer left, and its current discount would trigger it. EIM trades at a current yield of 5.9% and a discount of 8%.

In the third bucket, we have high-yield municipal funds, such as the mutual fund First Eagle High Yield Municipal Fund (FEHAX) or the CEF Nuveen Municipal Credit Income Fund (NZF). These funds allocate to high-yield / unrated tax-exempt bonds, which tend to carry a higher yield than the typically higher-quality tax-exempt options held by most funds. FEHAX has a current yield of 5.02% and modest leverage at 10%. FEHAX is run by John Miller - the so-called HY Muni king - who enjoyed a very strong run with his former flagship fund, NHMAX.

Finally, we continue to like term CEFs which could provide additional tailwind through discount amortization into a termination or a tender offer. We have had good results with term CEFs in the past, and they remain an important strategy in our Income Portfolios. Here we like the DWS Municipal Trust (KTF) and DTF Tax-Free Income 2028 Term Fund (DTF). KTF has a current yield of 7.6% and a likely additional 2% annual upside in discount amortization.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Southern Copper: Good For Holding In Long-Term, Not Enough For Buying Right Now
data.description:
I'm starting coverage of Southern Copper Corporation (NYSE:SCCO) with a hold rating. Despite its excellent financial performance and stable balance sheet, the company hasn't demonstrated enough growth sustainability to be considered as an outstanding company.

This means I'm holding my position, but not adding to it. If I weren't already a shareholder, I wouldn't consider initiating a position in SCCO at this time.

Thesis Explanation

Disclaimer: using the available hold rating, I still prefer the avoid / neutral / attractive coordinate system to evaluate the investment status of companies in the context of a long-term stock investment portfolio. Neutral status or hold rating relative to SCCO stocks is based solely on an analysis of the financial performance of the business without an assessment of the current value, although this analysis will also be carried out towards the end of the article. My opinion may be useful to investors considering whether to include the stock in a long-term portfolio and hold it for a 3–5 year horizon, and does not constitute a recommendation or trading instruction.

According to my quantitative rating system, SCCO scores 65 out of 100, but only stocks with a score of 70 or higher earn a "Buy" rating. For a stock to become an attractive capital allocation opportunity, I focus on three key characteristics: sustainable growth, strong solvency, and a competitive advantage. By assessing several financial indicators from the company's reporting, I ultimately obtain an overall weighted rating. Despite the greater weight of the Growth part in the final assessment, I begin a detailed analysis of the company with a business overview and financial performance analysis.

A Market Leader With A Strong Competitive Edge

Southern Copper is a large integrated full-cycle metal producer with deposits in Mexico and Peru. As the name suggests, the company's primary metal is copper, which accounts for about 75% of the business. SCCO is involved in the entire production cycle which consists of mining, smelting and refining. The remaining quarter is accounted for by the production of molybdenum, silver, and zinc.

A brief overview of the business is necessary to understand at least at a basic level what the company does, but the key point of the analysis is finding a competitive edge.

As of the end of 2024, SCCO produced 973,851 tons of copper with a 6.9% YoY growth rate pace. According to the Copper Council, this represents about 4.3% of the global copper market, which is estimated at 22.58 million tons.

Despite the relatively modest figures, which do not reach the double-digit level, Southern Copper can be included in the list of the largest producers of this metal in the world (it definitely makes it into the top 10 by volume).

Southern Copper has an effective business model that not only allows for economies of scale, but also is not so dependent on volatile market conditions. Proof of this is the high profitability metrics: gross margin of 57.7% TTM and net profit margin of 29.5%.

One of the company's financial strength is the $3.4 billion in net income for 2024, while cash flow from operations reached $4.4 billion, which indicates that cash flows correspond to (even exceed) accruals and signals high quality of profit and level of business model.

In conclusion of the analysis of the business model efficiency, I will give a metric that I calculate myself based on one of the available formulas of return on invested capital (inspired by Credit Suisse analysts method). Excluding cash and non-interest current liabilities from assets with the addition of a portion of revenue, my calculation leads to ROIC of 25%, which makes SCCO one of the most efficient companies in the industry.

As a brief summary:

  • SCCO is vertically integrated
  • It has high-quality proven reserves of over 67 million tons of copper
  • business is strongly efficient with competitive advantages, as evidenced by high levels of profitability indicators
  • Operating cash cost per pound of copper at $0.89 is one of the lowest in the industry.

Southern Copper Is Solvent And Liquid

In the Materials sector, credit is required to fully conduct operational activities, so the probability of finding a debt free company here tends to zero. However, the industry is cyclical, and the company's business depends on the rapidly changing global market conditions (from which SCCO is partially protected by a high level of margin). So managing the long-term load is important.

In my opinion, SCCO management is doing a good job. Current Assets are 2.7 times higher than current liabilities. Total debt, taking into account capital leases, is slightly less than $7b. Cash covers it only a half, but equity of $8.5b (12 months average) brings us to an acceptable value of 0.82 for debt-to-equity.

However, I pay more attention to other metrics, closer to reality. The first is cash flow from operations-to-debt as a kind of stress test equals to 0.63, and does not reach the desired level of 1 for full solvency. On the other hand, there is the second metric: interest expenses in the amount of $334m is only 6% of operating income, which over the past 12 months exceeded $5.5b. And this indicates that the debt burden is under control.

Growth Is Not Sustainable

So far, the analysis seems to suggest that SCCO stock is a viable investment, but when I get to the sustainable growth metrics, things are not so rosy.

Despite the fact that EPS expectations were not exceeded only 6 times in the last 16 quarters, 3-years CAGR is just 0.2%, and the net income growth rate which excludes the declining number of shares is completely negative—minus 0.2%.

This dynamics is partially offset by more optimistic expectations for the next 3-year period. EPS is expected to reach $4.66 in 2025, $4.56 in 2026 and $5.3 in 2027, which means a CAGR of 7%.

To compare the current clearly insufficient growth rates for an outstanding company with the expectations of positive dynamics and acceleration in 2027, I will use the 3rd measure of growth sustainability, my own one, which I call the Stability Ratio. This is the ratio of the average YoY growth over the past 12 quarters to the mathematical standard deviation of this sample. With an average value of 9%, the deviation is more than 44% and this is pretty high. I definitely cannot call such dynamics sustainable—the probability of deviation from the average is significant, and the size of the deviation is quite high.

The combination of these three factors makes me rate the growth sustainability as below average, and since the Growth factor is dominant in my rating system, it had a key influence on the final conclusion of hold rather than buy.

What could drive growth soon? On the micro level, SCCO's planned production expansion is forecast to be a key driver. Projects in Peru and Mexico could significantly increase output and revenue. The company aims to exceed 1 million tonnes of copper production per year, and given its proven operational efficiency, this could translate into higher earnings per share.

On the macro level, it could be a potential rate cut in 2024–2025. It could weaken the U.S. dollar, which typically supports higher commodity prices, including copper. While less certain, it could be a powerful impact for SCCO's profitability.

Lastly, while I'm not a dividend-focused investor, it's worth noting that SCCO's stable payout ratio of around 50% provides shareholders with consistent returns, currently offering a forward yield of just under 3%.

Risk Investing In SCCO

I am going to highlight two key risks in order of importance, in my opinion. The first one is commodity dependence. SCCO is essentially a quasi-commodity. Copper prices are volatile and are affected by a wide range of factors, from interest rates to industrial production pace in key consuming and producing regions. This makes SCCO's financial performance potential highly dependent on external market conditions.

Second, since we are talking about mining, regulatory risks cannot be underestimated. SCCO is exposed to significant regulatory challenges. Government policies, environmental regulations, and geopolitical instability in the regions where it operates could impact production costs and expansion plans, or even lead to unexpected disruptions. While these risks do not negate SCCO's strengths, they are critical factors that investors should keep in mind.

My Two Cents On The Current Valuation

Valuation is huge. But it comes last in my article only because its significance in the context of a particular investment or speculative strategy may change. I will share my quantitative approach, which is based on tracking of the dynamics of the market valuation immediately after the earnings release.

Over the past three years, the median price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio on the day after earnings release is 22.2x—almost identical to the current multiple. This also aligns closely with the average Street price target of $98.37.

I have every right to say that the stock is fairly priced. And that brings me to the overall conclusion.

Bottom Line

SCCO's business is efficient and stable, but earnings per share growth is highly dependent on market conditions and is therefore unstable. Since growth is a key factor in the investment attractiveness of assets for me, SCCO fell slightly short of a buy rating, but remains an asset in my long-term investment portfolio. At a price of around $60–65, I would increase my position without hesitation, but at the moment the company is fairly valued, and the position is not planned to change in any direction.

data.title: BLW: Too Expensive, Too Leveraged (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Thesis

The BlackRock Limited Duration Income Trust (NYSE:BLW) is a fixed-income closed end fund from BlackRock. The name focuses on leveraged loans and fixed rate bonds, and is a CEF we have covered before in the past. In our last article we highlighted the fund's low duration profile and attractive valuation profile via its discount to NAV, factors which have helped the name post a +25% total return since our last coverage.

In today's piece we are going to re-visit the name and its valuation metrics in the context of the current macro environment.

Blend of leveraged loans and bonds

BLW is fairly unique in the CEF space since it provides for an almost even split between leveraged loans and high yield bonds:

The CEF is a multi-asset vehicle, having positions in investment grade bonds and CLOs as well. However, as we can see from the above table, the main sleeves here are 'Term Loans' at 43%, and 'High Yield Bonds' at 41%. It is unusual to see this composition, since most names are either overweight floating rate loans or fixed rate bonds.

As the holdings suggest, the CEF is focused on below investment grade holdings:

  • AAA: 4%
  • AA: 8%
  • A: 2%
  • BBB: 9%
  • BB: 35%
  • B: 48%
  • CCC: 10%

Kindly note that the breakdown provided by BlackRock adds up to over 100% due to the embedded leverage and the fund calculation methodology. BLW in fact comes with a very high leverage ratio:

The CEF leverage ratio is 39%, which is unusual for a multi-asset name. Pure leveraged loan funds do tend to exhibit higher leverage ratios in the 30s, but blended ones like BLW are unusual. A high leverage translates into deeper drawdowns if a bear market develops. The CEF tries to balance this out with a low effective duration of only 2 years, but we can see via the WAL factor that the low duration mainly comes from the overweight floating rate loans positioning.

As an investor, you want to reduce leverage and beta when you are fearful of drawdowns, since they can result in significant negative performances and volatility. Just by way of comparison, names such as PGIM High Yield Bond Fund (ISD) have a 20% leverage ratio, while pure floating rate funds like Invesco Senior Income Trust (VVR) show a 32% leverage ratio. BLW's leverage is very high, and this is a concern for us in today's environment.

Discount to NAV - at the top of the historic range

The last time we covered this name, the valuation metrics looked very appealing, with the discount to NAV trading close to historic lows:

Currently we are witnessing the CEF record a premium to net asset value, an occurrence which is unusual for the fund. If we look back for ten years, we can see that only once in the past did we have a premium to NAV, and that was during the zero rates environment which characterized 2021.

We are big believers in mean-reversion, and that is how macroeconomic cycles usually behave. From that lens, top levels in valuations are to be sold, while bottoms are to be bought. We are looking at something close to the top currently. Therefore, we find it prudent to sell here. Long term buy-and-hold investors might argue with this view, but they can also cut some exposure while holding a core position.

High yield macro picture is not helpful

BLW's main risk driver is composed by credit spreads. While the CEF mitigates its rates component via a low duration, the fund is subject to a sudden widening in credit spreads. From a macro standpoint credit spreads are very, very tight currently:

As measured by the ICE BofA US High Yield Index, spreads are at a decade low. You read that correctly. If you look at the above chart carefully, you will notice they usually hovered above 3% historically, even in 2021. In today's world, they have broken to a low of 2.65%, basically providing only 265 bps over treasuries to compensate investors for taking high yield risk.

We find that figure to be very low, and indicates an overbought market for high yield. As an investor, you want to buy BLW when spreads are high (above 4% for example as per the chart), and reduce positioning or just hold when spreads are this low. While fundamentally the picture for the economy is still quite strong, entry points matter. And today's market is most certainly not a good entry point into high yield. BLW outperforms passive peers per its active and factor-based approach, but will not be able to escape a wide market sell-off when spreads blow up.

Distributions - reasonably high ROC usage

Another aspect to consider is the CEF's return of capital ('ROC') utilization. We usually look at names with ROC up to 30%, which we consider the upper range of an acceptable utilization. Kindly remember ROC is purely a return of your own principal. Thus, it does not represent a true yield from a fund. BLW's figure is 28% for the latest distribution:

While this figure is not egregious, it purely highlights that the fund's stated distribution rate of 9.5% is not supported, and the actual yield is closer to 7%. The risk reward here is therefore entirely skewed to the downside in our opinion, with all factors pointing to a mean reversion at some point in time. While none of the fund elements on its own is worrisome, the collection of all fund facts do point to an overvalued name in our view.

Conclusion

BLW is a fixed income closed end fund. The name contains a 50/50 split between leveraged loans and high yield bonds, and has a very high leverage ratio of 39%. While the name tries to balance this out with a low duration, the CEF still runs significant credit risk which will provide for a sharp negative performance during a bear market. With the fund now trading at a premium to NAV versus its usual discount and a high ROC figure of 28%, we see only downside from here. We are a 'Sell' for this CEF at the current levels.

data.title: Skechers Is Gaining Global Share While Building Performance But Trades At 12x P/E, It's A Buy
data.description:
Skechers (NYSE:SKX) released 4Q24 results, posting double-digit topline growth and achieving a 10% operating margin. The company’s U.S. wholesale business grew 31% YoY, benefiting from a restocking cycle at retailers. International sales also performed well, except for China, where the company probably is losing market share.

Despite cyclical margin risks, Skechers seems attractive at a 12x forward P/E. The company expects to grow MSD in the US and HSD abroad while building a strong performance offering that can give it pricing power. I think Skechers is a Buy at these levels.

Model recap

I presented a lengthier introduction to Skechers in my initiation article (Hold) from October 2024, back then I considered the company to be a strange mix of manufacturer (brand-heavy companies like Nike or Adidas, focused on wholesale) and retailer (the company has more than 5,000 locations between DOS and licensed stores). Skechers' brand is not as well regarded as other big sports brands like Nike or Adidas, but its products are considered good value for their price. I think this describes Skechers as a footwear retailer.

Despite not having the branding, Skechers' margins are competitive with those of large footwear manufacturers, and its earnings multiple is 1/3 of Nike's.

4Q24 results

Strong topline and margins: Skechers reported 12% revenue growth for FY 2024 and 13% in Q4. The company maintained a 10% operating margin, a little better YoY, in line with management’s long-term target.

Non-recurring US wholesale restocking: The U.S. wholesale business was the standout, growing 31% year over year in Q4. This was explained by retailers' cyclical restocking after a terrible destocking in 2022/23. This dynamic will not repeat in 2025, with management expecting a return to MSD growth in the U.S.

International at sustainable double digits: International wholesale also performed well, growing double digits across most regions. The EMEA region grew 25%, while APAC (excluding China) grew 26%.

Gaining market share: Among large global footwear manufacturers, Skechers is gaining market share. There is no large manufacturer of that scale growing at the same levels.

Skechers is able to grow because it offers more value (cheaper products of similar quality and lower branding) to segments of the population that may be more affected by inflation today. The company is also focusing on its wholesale channel (growing much more in the US and abroad versus DTC), contrary to competitors focusing more on DTC.

Performance keeps advancing: Skechers’ expansion into performance footwear (basketball, soccer, golf, pickleball) remains small but very promising. There were no specific figures on the call, but management clarified that the segment is still small versus lifestyle.

Still, when I review videos of shoe influencers in soccer, basketball, or golf, commends for Skechers offers are almost unanimous. Of course, the company does not compete in the more expensive categories (except maybe soccer) but provides very good value. One recent example is the Nexus line.

This is Skechers’ biggest long-term opportunity, as success in performance categories could enhance its brand perception and pricing power.

Search trends remain very positive in both the US and globally for 2025.

Quarterly negative points

China is a big question mark: China was the only weak spot, with revenue down 11% YoY in Q4. Importantly, this is not a broad-based market issue—the overall Chinese footwear market is not shrinking as fast, meaning Skechers is losing market share in the region. China is a key market not only because of its size but also because showing that the company can compete, there is important evidence that it will be able to compete with Chinese brands abroad.

Higher inventories: Inventories increased 26% YoY in Q4, well above sales. Management said this was caused by higher in-transit inventory due to shipping delays from the Red Sea/Suez Canal crisis. If demand softens, Skechers may need to become more promotional, impacting margins.

Higher effective taxes: Skechers’ effective tax rate is rising to 22% in 2025 due to the implementation of OECD global minimum tax laws.

Valuation is attractive

Skechers currently trades at $64 per share, but with $9.5 per share in net cash, its adjusted price is closer to $55 per share. At management’s 2025 EPS guidance of $4.30–$4.50, this translates to a 12.5x forward P/E.

For a company that is gaining market share, and therefore clearly doing something well, growing at MSD to HSD levels, and with the potential to expand margins via performance-driven pricing, this seems very attractive.

The main risk, in my opinion, is cyclical margin compression. If operating margins fall to 7–8% in a downturn, EPS could decline to ~$3.70 (on FY25 guided revenues of about $9.75 billion), implying a 15x P/E multiple at current prices. Not terrible, but a risk nonetheless.

For that reason, I believe Skechers is an opportunity, and a Buy at these prices.

data.title: Madison Small Cap Fund Q4 2024 Investment Strategy Letter
data.description:
The fourth quarter closed out a successful year for the Madison Small Cap Fund.

The fund delivered solid absolute and relative returns for the fourth quarter and the full year. Large caps continued to broadly outperform small caps and growth continued to outperform value. The AI boom marched on as Nvidia (NVDA) added $2 trillion to its market value in 2024. The recession in the cyclically exposed Industrial economy continued, as measured by the Purchasing Managers’ Index (PMI), even though treasury yields broke out to new highs. With the conclusion of the election, the market rallied, as the fog of policy uncertainty seemed to clear. However, by late November, new uncertainties around tariffs and inflation began to emerge, and the Russell 2000 Index gave back its entire post-election advance, finishing up a modest 0.33% for the quarter. The Madison Small Cap Fund (class I) returned 4.04% for the quarter, outperforming both the Russell 2000 Index (0.33%) and Russell 2500 Index (0.62%), driven by stock selection and favorable allocation.

Looking back at the full year, our outperformance versus our benchmarks was even more dramatic. Although the Russell 2000 Index was up 11.55% for the year, we outpaced our benchmark by a formidable 900 basis points, delivering a 22% total return. The overwhelming majority of our outperformance was a function of stock selection, although we did benefit from favorable sector allocation as well.

Portfolio Review

For the fourth quarter, our positive outperformance came from strong stock selection in Consumer Discretionary, Healthcare, and Information Technology, while Industrials, Financials, and Consumer Staples were drags to performance. We had one takeover in the portfolio as Summit Materials (SUM) announced they were being acquired by privately held Quikrete Holdings in an all-cash deal that represented a 30% premium to the unaffected stock price.

If we zoom out to the full year, we see a similar picture from a positive performance perspective, with Consumer Discretionary, Info Tech, and Healthcare driving outperformance. However, detractors were concentrated in Industrials and Communications Services.

In Industrials, one of our largest and oldest investments, Willscot Holdings Corp (WSC) had a difficult year. Not only did they walk from a transformative acquisition due to scrutiny from the FTC, but prolonged weakness in two of their most important end markets, nonresidential construction and residential construction, lead to slowing volumes and disappointing uptake in high margin, value-added products. We believe these issues are not due to competitive dynamics or poor execution. Rather, the current weakness is driven by the cyclically weak end market. Although we are disappointed that the planned acquisition of McGrath Rent Corp fell through, we believe the company is still the same high quality, durable business that has consistently gained market share while generating material free cash flow that it always was. The company has focused its efforts on organic growth initiatives and aggressively repurchasing stock. We have used this near-term weakness to add to our position.

Portfolio Activity

In the fourth quarter, we sold three stocks (FROG, GOGO, AMN) and added three new investments (KN, VFC, MEDP).

JFrog Ltd (FROG)

We sold our position in Software vendor JFrog. While we continue to feel this is a strategic asset in the critical software development automation market, we've grown increasingly uncomfortable with management's credibility and visibility into its business. In the final days of the second quarter (3 business days), in conjunction with an acquisition, the company reiterated its quarterly and annual guidance. It then proceeded to miss on both metrics only weeks later during the second quarter.

Gogo Inc. (GOGO)

Our thesis that the company would resolve its issues with 5G technology and provide a viable service was broken, and we decided to exit the position.

AMN Healthcare Services (AMN)

Temporary nurse staffing company AMN Healthcare’s fundamentals have been challenged since 2022. During the pandemic, the company’s billing rates reached record highs. As health services normalized, so did their billing rates. Although we believed we’d reached a trough in the company’s valuation and fundamentals, we were wrong. We’ve decided to exit the position and reassess the business as we uncover further evidence of industry stabilization.

Knowles Corporation (KN)

We established a new investment position in Knowles Corp. Knowles supplies advanced micro-acoustic, audio processing, and precision device solutions. The underlying technologies are capacitors and RF filters. The company recently sold its low-margin consumer-facing business, which sold components into Apple’s ear pods, the proceeds to pay down debt and reinvest in higher margin, more durable Industrial and medical businesses that should command a higher valuation. We believe the moats in their industrial and medical business are substantial and believe the intrinsic value for the stock is $27.

VF Corporation (VFC)

We initiated a new investment position in VF Corporation (~8$b market cap). Formerly known as Vanity Fair, VF is the owner of a portfolio of apparel and footwear brands, namely Vans, The North Face, and Timberlands, which make up the lion’s share of the revenues. VFC is in a special situation with relatively new management, executing a turnaround strategy that we believe is value-maximizing and very shareholder-friendly. The company recently sold one of its portfolio brands, Supreme, for $1.5b and used the proceeds to pay down debt. They’ve reduced costs across the portfolio, including shrinking the Van’s retail business.

Although margins have improved from the recent trough as a result of these actions, there is ample room for expansion, and we believe the portfolio can be further rationalized. We would not be surprised if Timberland gets sold to further de-lever the balance sheet, leaving the company focused on two strong brands that have, we believe, room to grow mostly in international markets but also domestically. We further believe a fully rationalized portfolio with a cleaner balance sheet alongside a recovery in the Vans business could improve the intrinsic value, which we currently estimate to be $28 per share.

Medpace Holdings, Inc. (MEDP)

Medpace is the leading CRO (contract research organization) with a focused model on the Biopharma industry. The focus on small and medium-sized biopharmaceutical companies leads to customer intimacy, consistent execution, and fixed pricing, which are the keys to the company's moats and consistent market share gains over the last 10 years. Despite large swings in biotech funding, MEDP has driven sequentially positive revenue growth since 2015 and continues to boast an impressive backlog of business, which we believe can drive durable growth going forward. MEDP has a pristine balance sheet, generates healthy free cash flow (20%+ margins on earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization), and is opportunistic with stock buybacks. We estimate intrinsic value at $450.

Outlook

After a brutal 2022, the market and our fund have rebounded nicely. Small caps were able to briefly recapture their 2021 peak but were unable to breakthrough. Meanwhile, the S&P 500, led by the Magnificent 7 broke the 2021 peak early in 2024 and continue to make new highs. Over the last several years, we’ve shared data highlighting the attractiveness of small caps both as relative and absolute values. Although small caps continue to represent good relative value, the small cap market is no longer broadly “cheap.” However, there are almost always pockets of value or pockets of opportunity—which is why strong stock selection will be even more crucial. There are several questions that we’re pondering that we believe are worth sharing.

Will the AI boom continue?

Over the last five years, the five hyperscalers (Google (GOOG)(GOOGL), Amazon (AMZN), Microsoft (MSFT), Meta (META), and Oracle (ORCL)) have spent nearly $1 trillion dollars on capital spending. Quite a lot of these dollars have fueled the massive growth in Nvidia’s business and valuation. An ecosystem of other technologies and businesses has grown alongside Nvidia, including some in our portfolio of investments. Over the last few months, some investors have wondered if there’s an actual economic return from these investments.

While it’s too early to say, we will be looking for actual applications beyond just ChatGPT and generative AI to materialize to justify this spend. If investors lose confidence in Big Tech’s ability to generate a real economic return, the boom could easily turn into bust.

When will housing recover?

Housing is one of the most important subsectors of the economy that’s been challenged for at least two years. High housing costs and high interest rates have been a significant headwind to activity. The new administration’s ability to tackle this issue will be important to watch, although solutions could be complex and drawn out. Although the fed has eased back on short term rates, we have not seen a meaningful retreat in mortgage rates.

Will 2025 bring a broad industrial recovery?

The Purchasing Managers’ Index (PMI) is a widely used economic indicator that assesses the health of a country’s manufacturing or services sector. Indications below 50 signal contraction and the PMI has been below 50 for 21 months. Although quite a bit of this is inventory absorption from the COVID supply shocks, weakness in housing and nonresidential construction have been negative contributing factors.

With these questions in mind, we enter 2025 very cautiously. We reiterate there are always opportunities in the vast reservoir of small cap stocks, but the challenge, as always, is to find quality and durable businesses at attractive valuations.

Faraz Farzam | Aaron Garcia
